pageName: TiDB vs MongoDB: SQL Scalability & ACID Consistency

meta.title: TiDB vs MongoDB: SQL Scalability & ACID Consistency
meta.description: Compare TiDB and MongoDB. TiDB offers distributed SQL with strong ACID consistency, real-time analytics, and automatic scaling—no shard-key complexity.

props.headline: TiDB vs MongoDB
props.subheadline: A distributed SQL alternative that delivers strong ACID consistency, SQL for complex queries, and real-time analytics on live operational data—without shard-key-driven scaling or multi-system sprawl.

props.title: How TiDB Compares to MongoDB
props.subtitle: A side-by-side comparison across the architectural and operational dimensions that matter most as applications scale.

rows.ours: Distributed SQL with decoupled compute and storage. Automatic data partitioning and replication across nodes.
rows.theirs: Document database with collections and BSON. Scaling via replica sets and optional sharded clusters.

rows.ours: Horizontal scale-out for both reads and writes. Automatic rebalancing without application sharding.
rows.theirs: Read scaling via replicas. Write scaling via sharding, which requires careful shard key selection.

rows.ours: Standard SQL with joins, aggregations, window functions, and relational modeling.
rows.theirs: Document query language and aggregation pipeline. Cross-collection queries require denormalization.

rows.ours: Strong ACID transactions across the cluster by default using distributed consensus (Raft).
rows.theirs: Multi-document transactions supported. Consistency depends on read/write concerns and patterns.

rows.ours: Native HTAP: OLTP and OLAP on same dataset with real-time replication. No ETL required.
rows.theirs: Analytics via separate systems (data warehouse, Atlas Data Federation, or external pipelines).

rows.ours: Relational schema with optional JSON columns. Supports structured relationships and constraints.
rows.theirs: Flexible schema by default. Document-centric modeling with denormalization for performance.

rows.ours: Automatic data partitioning and rebalancing. No manual shard key selection required.
rows.theirs: Requires selecting a shard key. Poor shard key design creates hotspots and operational overhead.

rows.ours: Automated placement, replication, and failover. Minimal application intrusion as scale increases.
rows.theirs: Requires decisions on shard keys, replica set configuration, and cluster tuning as scale grows.

rows.ours: Proven support for large SaaS architectures with high schema/table counts and strong isolation.
rows.theirs: Multi-tenancy supported but large tenant counts and shard growth increase operational overhead.

rows.ours: Strong consistency across all distributed nodes by default.
rows.theirs: Tunable consistency via read preference and write concern settings.

props.title: Architectural Differences That Matter
props.subtitle: How TiDB's distributed SQL design compares to MongoDB's document and sharding model.

items.title: Complex Queries in One System
items.description: TiDB supports joins, aggregations, and relational queries natively with SQL. In MongoDB, complex relationships often require denormalization or added application complexity.

items.title: Strong Consistency for Critical Workflows
items.description: TiDB provides strong ACID guarantees by default across distributed nodes. MongoDB offers tunable consistency, requiring explicit read/write concerns for correctness at scale.

items.title: Scale Without Shard-Key Lock-In
items.description: TiDB automatically distributes and rebalances data as the cluster grows. MongoDB sharding requires careful shard key selection and can be costly to change later.

items.title: Real-Time Analytics on Live Data
items.description: TiDB runs transactional and analytical workloads on the same dataset, enabling operational insights without data movement. MongoDB analytics typically shift to external systems.

items.title: Lower System Sprawl Over Time
items.description: TiDB reduces the need for separate stores for analytics and complex querying. MongoDB deployments often add additional systems for reporting and analytics as requirements expand.

items.title: Unified Data Foundation
items.description: TiDB consolidates transactional and analytical workloads into a single distributed system. MongoDB often requires pairing with separate analytics and search systems.

props.title: When to Choose TiDB vs MongoDB
props.subtitle: The right choice depends on how much relational complexity, correctness, and real-time insight your application needs.

items.title: Choose TiDB if you need:
items.description: Strong consistency for complex, multi-entity workflows with ACID by default. SQL for joins, aggregations, and rich querying. Real-time analytics on operational data without ETL. Scale-out reads and writes without shard-key decisions. A single distributed database foundation that evolves with your product.

items.title: MongoDB may be a fit if:
items.description: Your data is primarily document-centric with minimal relational querying. You prioritize flexible schema and dynamic document structures. You have existing MongoDB investments and team expertise. Your workload patterns favor document-oriented access patterns over SQL.

props.title: Ready to Experience Distributed SQL at Scale?
props.subtitle: Start with TiDB Cloud today and see how strong consistency, automatic scaling, and real-time analytics can simplify your architecture.
